questions.question: Explain with examples how polynomials are classified based on the number of terms.
questions.answer: Polynomials are classified based on the number of terms as monomials (one term), binomials (two terms) and trinomials (three terms). For example, 5x is a monomial, x + 3 is a binomial and x² + 2x + 1 is a trinomial.

questions.question: Explain why x⁻² + 3x is not a polynomial.
questions.answer: The expression x⁻² + 3x is not a polynomial because it contains a variable with a negative power. In a polynomial, the powers of variables must be non-negative integers.

questions.question: Explain the relationship between the degree of a polynomial and the shape of its graph.
questions.answer: The degree of a polynomial determines the general shape of its graph. A degree one polynomial has a straight-line graph, degree two has a parabolic graph, and higher-degree polynomials have more curves.

questions.question: Explain how the value of a polynomial is found at a given point.
questions.answer: To find the value of a polynomial at a given point, substitute the value of the variable into the polynomial and simplify the expression.

questions.question: Explain with a graph how zeroes of a polynomial are obtained.
questions.answer: Zeroes of a polynomial are obtained from the graph by finding the x-coordinates where the graph intersects the x-axis. These points indicate where the polynomial value becomes zero.

questions.question: Explain why a linear polynomial can have only one zero.
questions.answer: A linear polynomial has degree one and its graph is a straight line. A straight line can intersect the x-axis at only one point, so it can have only one zero.

questions.question: Explain why a quadratic polynomial may have two, one or no zeroes.
questions.answer: A quadratic polynomial has a parabolic graph. Depending on whether the parabola cuts, touches or does not intersect the x-axis, it may have two, one or no zeroes.

questions.question: Explain the importance of arranging a polynomial in standard form.
questions.answer: Arranging a polynomial in standard form helps in easily identifying its degree, leading coefficient and type of polynomial.

questions.question: Explain why the degree of a non-zero constant polynomial is zero.
questions.answer: A non-zero constant polynomial has no variable term. Since the highest power of the variable is zero, its degree is zero.

questions.question: Explain why a polynomial cannot have fractional powers of variables.
questions.answer: Fractional powers violate the definition of a polynomial, which requires all exponents of variables to be non-negative integers.

questions.question: Explain how the number of zeroes of a polynomial is related to its degree using examples.
questions.answer: The maximum number of zeroes of a polynomial is equal to its degree. For example, a linear polynomial has at most one zero and a quadratic polynomial has at most two zeroes.

questions.question: Explain why the graph of a polynomial is continuous.
questions.answer: The graph of a polynomial is continuous because it has no breaks or gaps for any real value of the variable.

questions.question: Explain how polynomials are useful in modelling real-life situations.
questions.answer: Polynomials are used to model relationships involving area, profit and loss, speed, distance and other real-life situations with predictable patterns.

questions.question: Explain why the zero polynomial has no defined degree.
questions.answer: The zero polynomial has all coefficients equal to zero, so there is no highest power of the variable to determine its degree.

questions.question: Explain the role of coefficients in determining the value of a polynomial.
questions.answer: Coefficients determine the contribution of each term in the polynomial and influence the value of the polynomial for different variable values.

questions.question: Explain why a polynomial of degree three can have at most three zeroes.
questions.answer: Because the degree of the polynomial is three, and the maximum number of zeroes possible equals the degree.

questions.question: Explain how graphs help in comparing different polynomials.
questions.answer: Graphs help compare polynomials by showing their shapes, intercepts, and relative positions on the coordinate plane.

questions.question: Explain why every polynomial is an algebraic expression but not every algebraic expression is a polynomial.
questions.answer: All polynomials are algebraic expressions, but algebraic expressions containing negative or fractional powers are not polynomials.

questions.question: Explain why the x-intercepts of a polynomial graph represent its zeroes.
questions.answer: At x-intercepts, the value of y is zero, meaning the polynomial equals zero at those x-values.

questions.question: Explain how the leading coefficient affects the graph of a polynomial.
questions.answer: The leading coefficient affects the direction and steepness of the graph of the polynomial.

questions.question: Explain the importance of polynomials in higher mathematics.
questions.answer: Polynomials form the basis for equations, functions, calculus and many advanced mathematical concepts.

questions.question: Explain how polynomial functions differ from rational functions.
questions.answer: Polynomial functions have only non-negative integer powers, while rational functions may contain variables in denominators.

questions.question: Explain why polynomials are easier to graph than other algebraic expressions.
questions.answer: Polynomials are continuous and smooth, making their graphs easier to draw and interpret.

questions.question: Explain how zeroes of polynomials help in solving equations.
questions.answer: Zeroes help identify values of variables that satisfy polynomial equations.

questions.question: Explain the role of polynomials in mathematical modelling.
questions.answer: Polynomials help represent and analyze relationships between variables in real-life models.
